title: MSTY Now Pays Weekly: YieldMax's MicroStrategy ETF Shifts to Weekly Distributions as AUM Tops $1 Billion
description: YieldMax's MSTY ETF has switched from monthly to weekly dividend distributions. With $1B+ in assets and yields near 100%, here's what investors need to know about the shift and the risks.
lede: The YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY) has quietly shifted from monthly to weekly distributions in 2026, paying out between $0.30 and $0.44 per share every single week. With assets under management surpassing $1 billion and an annualized yield that fluctuates around 90-100%, MSTY has become the most-watched covered call ETF in the market — and the most debated.

faqs.q: How often does MSTY pay dividends in 2026?
faqs.a: MSTY switched from monthly to weekly distributions in January 2026. Investors now receive payouts every week, with recent amounts ranging from $0.30 to $0.44 per share.

faqs.q: What is MSTY's current dividend yield?
faqs.a: Based on recent weekly distributions averaging roughly $0.37/week and a share price around $21, MSTY's annualized yield is approximately 90-100%. However, most of this is return of capital, not true income.

faqs.q: Why is MSTY's share price declining despite high dividends?
faqs.a: MSTY uses a covered call strategy that caps upside but absorbs full downside from MicroStrategy stock moves. Additionally, 90-100% of distributions are classified as return of capital (ROC), meaning the fund is largely paying investors back their own money while NAV erodes.

faqs.q: Is MSTY a good long-term investment?
faqs.a: MSTY is designed for income generation, not long-term capital appreciation. Its NAV has declined significantly since inception despite massive distributions. Total return (distributions + price change) has been positive, but requires active management and reinvestment discipline.

faqs.q: What does return of capital mean for MSTY investors?
faqs.a: Return of capital (ROC) means the distribution is not generated from investment income but is a return of the investor's own investment. Recent MSTY distributions have been 90-100% ROC, which reduces the cost basis and defers taxes but reflects NAV erosion.

sections.heading: The Shift to Weekly Payouts
paragraphs: Starting in January 2026, MSTY began distributing income weekly rather than monthly. The four most recent distributions — $0.3448, $0.4383, $0.3847, and $0.3500 per share — show the weekly cadence in action. For an investor holding 1,000 shares, that's roughly $300-440 hitting their account every single week.

The shift reflects both investor demand for more frequent income and YieldMax's broader strategy of positioning its covered-call ETFs as cash flow machines. MSTY isn't the only fund making the switch — several YieldMax products now pay weekly, including TSLY (Tesla) and NVDY (Nvidia).

sections.heading: The Return of Capital Problem
paragraphs: Here's what most MSTY investors don't fully grasp: recent distributions have been 90-100% return of capital (ROC). The March 18 payout was 98.82% ROC. The February 25 payout was 100% ROC. That means the fund is essentially paying investors back their own money while the net asset value erodes.

This isn't necessarily a scam — it's how covered call strategies work when the underlying asset is volatile. MSTY generates income by selling call options on MicroStrategy, but when MSTR's price drops, the fund absorbs the full loss while having already capped its upside through the sold calls. The result is a slow grind lower in NAV punctuated by massive weekly payouts that feel like income but are partially capital depletion.
Track the full distribution history, ROC percentages, and NAV erosion with our MSTY Dividend History & Analysis tool — updated with every weekly payout.

sections.heading: What's Actually in the Fund
paragraphs: MSTY's holdings reveal a surprise: the fund is 47% cash and equivalents, with another 52% in U.S. Treasury Bills maturing between April and September 2026. The actual MicroStrategy exposure comes from just 4.2% of the portfolio allocated to MSTR call options at the $135 and $140 strikes.

This means MSTY is effectively a Treasury Bill fund with a small options overlay. The massive distributions come from selling those call options — collecting premiums from traders betting on MSTR's price movements. When MicroStrategy is volatile (which is most of the time, given its massive Bitcoin holdings), those premiums are enormous.

sections.heading: Total Return vs Yield Illusion
paragraphs: Since its February 2024 inception, MSTY's cumulative NAV return is +66.96% (annualized 28.90%). That includes all distributions reinvested. The S&P 500 returned +41.77% over the same period. So MSTY has outperformed — but with dramatically higher volatility and risk.

The 2026 story is different: MSTY is down 12.17% year-to-date on a market price basis, and down 43.28% over the trailing 12 months. Investors who bought at the highs and didn't reinvest distributions are sitting on significant losses. The headline yield masks the price destruction.

For investors evaluating whether MSTY fits their portfolio, compare it against other income strategies using our Monthly Dividend Stocks comparison — which includes 40+ monthly and weekly payers sorted by yield, type, and risk profile.

sections.heading: Who Should (and Shouldn't) Own MSTY
paragraphs: MSTY makes sense for investors who understand what they're buying: a volatility harvesting vehicle, not a traditional dividend stock. Successful MSTY investors typically take the weekly distributions and immediately reinvest them into stable assets — essentially using MSTY as an income engine that funds diversification.

It does not make sense for retirees who need stable, reliable income. It does not make sense for passive investors who won't monitor NAV erosion. And it definitely doesn't make sense in a taxable account for high earners — most distributions are taxed as ordinary income at your highest marginal rate. An IRA or Roth IRA is the better home for this kind of strategy.
